headline: Chips Lead a Risk-On Rally as Geopolitical Fears Ebb
body_html:
Stocks closed broadly higher Monday as easing tensions in the Middle East emboldened buyers and breathed new life into the technology complex. Advancers outpaced decliners 111 to 88 across our universe of 199 names, a tilt that confirmed the session's constructive tone even as the gains were unevenly distributed across sectors.

Semiconductors set the pace, the group surging 2.35 percent to lead all sectors as chip-equipment names rebounded sharply. Materials followed with a 1.15 percent advance, and software added 0.76 percent. The rotation was decidedly toward cyclicals and growth, leaving the more defensive corners behind. Homebuilders bucked the trend entirely, sliding 1.29 percent, while consumer names slipped 0.13 percent.

The standout belonged to the glass and materials maker that jumped 15.67 percent, the day's biggest gainer and a reminder that single-name catalysts can still eclipse the broader move. Chip-equipment shares posted double-digit advances, and a leading cybersecurity name climbed 9.14 percent. Yet the strength was not universal: steel producers and aggregates names retreated, underscoring that even on a green day, the market remains selective about where it places its conviction.

briefs.headline: Glass Maker Tops the Tape
briefs.body: The session's most striking move came from a materials and glass producer that surged 15.67 percent, vaulting it to the top of the gainers list. The rally stood apart from the broader materials group, which advanced a more modest 1.15 percent, highlighting a company-specific catalyst at work.

briefs.headline: Chip Equipment Roars Back
briefs.body: Semiconductor-equipment names led the day's rebound, with two of the group climbing 11.97 percent and 10.82 percent respectively. The advance powered the semiconductor sector to a 2.35 percent gain, the strongest of any group as buyers returned to the chip complex.

briefs.headline: Middle East Tensions Ease
briefs.body: Reports that US and Iranian negotiators were heading toward talks in Doha helped calm markets, even as the meeting remained uncertain. The de-escalation underpinned a risk-on tone, lifting equities broadly as fears of further regional disruption faded through the session.

briefs.headline: Steel Names Buck the Advance
briefs.body: Not every corner participated in the rally. Two steel producers fell 4.67 percent and 4.59 percent, while an aggregates maker dropped 5.65 percent. The weakness in heavy-industry materials cut against the broader green tape and weighed on the cyclical narrative.

briefs.headline: Cybersecurity Shares Climb
briefs.body: A leading cybersecurity name advanced 9.14 percent as the software sector added 0.76 percent. The move reflected renewed appetite for the group, which has drawn attention amid persistent demand for digital defense and a steadier backdrop for technology spending.

briefs.headline: Homebuilders Lag the Field
briefs.body: Homebuilders were the day's weakest sector, retreating 1.29 percent even as most groups finished higher. The decline left the housing-linked names as a notable laggard, a divergence worth watching as traders weigh the path of rates and consumer demand.
